prior.title: Washington State Commerce Department Receives 91 Applications for Multifamily Housing Preservation Programs
prior.summary: The Washington State Department of Commerce received 91 applications totaling $97.7 million for housing preservation and affordability programs, with $34.1 million available funding. The programs aim to preserve or maintain affordability for over 3,200 housing units across Washington. Award decisions are expected in June. Additionally, the Commerce Department appointed Antoinette Sullivan as Community Engagement Division assistant director, bringing 15+ years of international development experience.
prior.full_brief:
# Washington State Commerce Department: 91 Applications for Multifamily Housing Preservation Programs

**Market Signal Brief | Initiative | MONITORING**

---

## Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief covers the Washington State Department of Commerce's 2026 Housing Preservation Program funding round, in which 91 applications have been submitted. It is relevant to workspaces operating in the Pacific Northwest corridor with interests in logistics, real estate services, last-mile delivery, or construction supply chains adjacent to affordable housing development. The brief surfaces the funding volume, participant pool, program structure, and forward timeline as first-class intelligence. Awards are pending; re-check window: late summer/fall 2026 (see Section 3 for monitoring trigger).

Convention: all claims are labeled FACT, ANALYSIS, or LEGAL per the provenance contract. Binding-obligation verbs in unlabeled prose are not used.

---

# What's Moving and What Triggered It

**MONITORING**

The Washington State Department of Commerce's Multifamily Housing Unit (MHU) is running a 2026 Housing Preservation Program funding round. The program generated 91 applications.

FACT: The Commerce funding opportunities page lists "See the list of 2026 Housing Preservation applications (PDF)" as a published document, confirming the application round closed and the list of applicants is publicly available. *Source: Funding Opportunities for Multifamily Rental Housing, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/multifamily-funding/

FACT: The Housing Preservation Program "makes funds available for major building improvements, preservation renovations or upgrades, and system replacements necessary for existing Commerce-funded multi-family rental projects." *Source: Housing Preservation Program, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/housing-preservation/

FACT: The stated purpose of the Housing Preservation Program is "to maintain long-term viability of the existing housing portfolio." *Source: Housing Preservation Program, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/housing-preservation/

FACT: The Kent Reporter confirmed that "Kent multifamily housing complexes apply for state grants," identifying at least one municipality (Kent, WA) with applicants in this round. *Source: Kent Reporter, "Kent Multifamily Housing Complexes Apply for State Grants," May 7, 2026. https://www.kentreporter.com/2026/05/07/kent-multifamily-housing-complexes-apply-for-state-grants/

FACT: The 2025–2027 Biennial Investment Strategy "details the Multifamily Housing Unit's plan to award $419 million that has been allocated by the state legislature, over the next two years." *Source: Funding Opportunities for Multifamily Rental Housing, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/multifamily-funding/

FACT: Governor Ferguson announced "historic investments in affordable housing" via executive order. *Source: Office of Governor Ferguson, "Governor Ferguson Announces Historic Investments in Affordable Housing," 2025. https://governor.wa.gov/news/2025/governor-ferguson-announces-historic-investments-affordable-housing-executive-order-help-establish

Analytical inference: The combination of a $419 million biennial allocation, a gubernatorial executive order framing affordable housing as a political priority, and 91 preservation applications in a single round indicates that Washington State's housing capital deployment is in an elevated activity phase — above baseline preservation levels observed in prior biennia.

---

# Who's Driving It and What They Want

FACT: The Washington State Department of Commerce, through its Multifamily Housing Unit, administers the Housing Preservation Program and makes competitive funding awards to qualifying applicants. *Source: Housing Preservation Program, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/housing-preservation/

FACT: Eligible applicants described in Commerce program documentation include "community-based organizations, tribes, housing authorities, and local governments." *Source: Funding Opportunities for Multifamily Rental Housing, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/multifamily-funding/

FACT: Commerce states it is "particularly focused on serving organizations that arise out of such communities with the intention to service them — sometimes referred to as 'By and For Organizations,'" including rural community members, BIPOC community members, LGBTQ+ community members, and other historically marginalized groups. *Source: Funding Opportunities for Multifamily Rental Housing, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/multifamily-funding/

FACT: In April 2024, Commerce awarded "$19.8 million awards will help make essential repairs in 14 counties across the state" and "preserve and repair 873 units of affordable housing" through the prior Housing Preservation round. *Source: Housing Preservation Program, Washington State Department of Commerce, page updated April 17, 2024. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/housing-preservation/

Analytical inference: The 2024 precedent — 873 units, 14 counties, $19.8 million — establishes a baseline scale for what the 2026 round may mobilize, though the total available in 2026 has not been independently confirmed from the sourced blocks. The $419 million biennial envelope covers all MHU programs, not the preservation program alone; the preservation-specific allocation for 2026 is a research gap (see below).

*Specific preservation-program funding allocation for the 2026 round not available from primary sources as of May 2026.*

---

# Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

MONITORING — re-check window: late summer/fall 2026

FACT: The Commerce multifamily funding page references the "2026 Housing Preservation funding notice and application materials" and "See the list of 2026 Housing Preservation applications (PDF)" as published, indicating the application intake phase is complete and the award review phase is under way. *Source: Funding Opportunities for Multifamily Rental Housing, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/multifamily-funding/

FACT: The 2023 Housing Preservation Program awards were "announced on March 27, 2024," roughly five months after the prior-year application cycle. *Source: Funding Opportunities for Multifamily Rental Housing, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/multifamily-funding/

FACT: The 2025 Housing Trust Fund awarded projects were "announced on January 15, 2026." *Source: Funding Opportunities for Multifamily Rental Housing, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/multifamily-funding/

Analytical inference: Based on the pattern of prior award announcements — ranging from January to March following application intake — 2026 Housing Preservation award decisions are likely to be published in a late-2026 or early-2027 window. This is an estimate from observed program cadence, not a stated schedule. Set a re-check for December 2026.

---

# Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

Filtered by transport mode and cargo vertical relevant to workspaces with Pacific Northwest operations.

Analytical inference: For workspaces operating in air-primary freight with high-value or time-critical cargo verticals, this program is structurally indirect — housing preservation grants fund building improvements and system replacements at existing multifamily properties, not infrastructure with direct air-freight dependencies.

Analytical inference: For workspaces with road-mode operations in Washington State — particularly last-mile delivery, construction supply logistics, or drayage supporting building-materials distribution — a 91-application preservation round represents a foreseeable demand signal. Preservation work (major building improvements, system replacements) generates construction materials movement concentrated in urban and suburban King, Pierce, Snohomish, and surrounding counties. Kent's confirmed participation (sourced from Kent Reporter) indicates activity in a logistics-dense south King County corridor.

Analytical inference: Workspaces providing logistics for the construction and building-materials supply chain — including HVAC, roofing, plumbing, flooring, and mechanical systems — should monitor award announcements as a project-pipeline indicator. Award size, county distribution, and project timelines will determine whether individual preservation projects generate freight volumes worth direct pursuit.

Analytical inference: For workspaces with a humanitarian or community-sector vertical, or those serving non-profit housing developers as clients, the 91-application volume signals an active procurement environment among non-profit housing operators — a potential entry point for services addressing move management, equipment delivery, or building-supply logistics for funded projects.

---

# Competitive Implications

*No competitor-specific sourced reporting was identified in the available source blocks. Competitor positioning for this item requires trade reporting beyond what is provided.*

Analytical inference: At 91 applications for a single preservation funding round, the concentration of non-profit and housing-authority applicants in Washington State indicates a competitive funding environment among applicants themselves — not among logistics operators. For workspaces in road freight or construction logistics, the award decisions will determine which projects activate and which do not; early supplier relationships with funded applicants create a sourcing advantage before formal procurement launches.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- Washington State is running an active 2026 housing preservation funding cycle with 91 applications submitted and awards pending under a $419 million biennial housing allocation.
- The preservation program targets existing Commerce-funded multifamily properties for major building improvements and system replacements — a defined, recurring project type with known material and logistics demands.
- Prior rounds (2024: $19.8 million, 873 units, 14 counties) establish the scale of activated project work that follows award announcements.

**What to avoid saying:**

- Do not assert a specific dollar figure for the 2026 preservation round — the program-specific allocation has not been confirmed from the available sourced material.
- Do not assert award timing beyond "consistent with prior-year patterns" — no official timeline for 2026 award announcements has been sourced.
- Do not cite the 91-application figure as representing a specific number of units or a specific funding amount — those details are not confirmed from the sourced blocks.

**Questions to pose to clients in affected sectors:**

- Which awarded projects fall within your existing service footprint (Kent corridor, south King County, Pierce, Snohomish)?
- Does your procurement cycle allow you to pre-qualify with non-profit housing developers before award announcements?
- Are you tracking the Commerce MHU award list, which is published as a public PDF at award announcement?

---

# What the Workspace Should Do Now

**MONITORING**

1. **Subscribe to Commerce MHU award notifications.** The Commerce website offers email and SMS subscription for news updates. Set an alert for the 2026 Housing Preservation award announcement. No cost. Applicable to any workspace with road-freight or construction-logistics exposure in Washington State. Act now — award review is likely in progress.

2. **Download and analyze the 2026 Housing Preservation applications list.** The list is publicly available as a PDF linked from the Commerce multifamily funding page. Reviewing applicant names and geographies identifies which operators are likely to receive awards and whether they are existing or prospective clients.
   *Source: Funding Opportunities for Multifamily Rental Housing, Washington State Department of Commerce, accessed May 2026. https://www.commerce.wa.gov/multifamily-rental-housing/multifamily-funding/

3. **Flag Kent, WA and south King County as an active demand corridor.** Kent Reporter reporting confirms local multifamily complexes are applicants. The Kent area is a logistics hub; any funded preservation projects there generate proximity advantages for workspaces with existing Kent-area operations.

4. **Re-check December 2026.** The forward-intelligence monitor for this item is set to December 2026, consistent with observed award-announcement cadence. If awards are announced earlier, pull the Commerce MHU award PDF at that time.

5. *Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any applicable state procurement or vendor-registration requirement governs contracting with award recipients for construction logistics services would require review by counsel familiar with Washington State procurement rules for publicly funded non-profit housing projects.

prior.title: WSDOT Clark County Highway Cleanup Initiative: 3,410 lbs Debris Removed, $37K Staff Time Investment
prior.summary: Washington State Department of Transportation conducted a weeklong cleanup effort across Clark County highways (I-5, I-205, SR 14, SR 500, SR 503) during Earth Week 2026, removing 3,410 pounds of roadside litter with 49 staff members at an estimated cost of $37,194 in labor. The initiative highlights growing roadside debris challenges and emphasizes shared responsibility for highway cleanliness and safety.
prior.full_brief:
# WSDOT Clark County Highway Cleanup Initiative: 3,410 lbs Debris Removed, $37K Staff Time Investment

**MONITORING** | Format: Market Signal Brief | Signal Band: Corporate

---

## Preliminary Note

This event — a state DOT highway cleanup during Earth Week 2026 — is a government maintenance and community-engagement activity, not a commercial freight market signal. The workspace profile drives transport operations across air, road, and ocean modes. The connection to workspace operations exists via: (a) road freight dependency on I-5/I-205 and SR corridors in Clark County, Washington; (b) cargo security and load-securing obligations that generate roadside debris; and (c) the broader signal about litter-driven highway maintenance budgets and cost pressure on state road operations that intersect with freight-moving infrastructure.

This brief is structured to extract the operationally relevant signal for freight operations using these corridors, not to report the cleanup as an end in itself.

---

# What's Moving and What Triggered It

**Background — the corridor.** WSDOT maintenance crews and agency staff conducted a weeklong litter removal operation along Clark County highway corridors during Earth Week (week of April 20, 2026). *Source: WSDOT News Release, WSDOT, May 7, 2026. https://wsdot.wa.gov/about/news/2026/cleanup-aisle-i-5-crews-haul-away-litter-along-clark-county-highways-during-earth-week.

FACT: The cleanup covered "several stretches of Interstate 5, I-205, State Route 14, SR 500 and SR 503." *Source: WSDOT News Release, WSDOT, May 7, 2026. https://wsdot.wa.gov/about/news/2026/cleanup-aisle-i-5-crews-haul-away-litter-along-clark-county-highways-during-earth-week. These routes are primary freight corridors connecting the Portland, Oregon metropolitan area to markets in southwest Washington and beyond, carrying significant road freight volume for workspaces operating north–south US West Coast lanes.

FACT: The operation removed "3,410 pounds of trash" and involved "49 members of WSDOT staff joined agency maintenance crews and volunteers from agency partners." *Source: AASHTO Journal, AASHTO, May 15, 2026. https://aashtojournal.transportation.org/wsdot-crews-volunteers-collect-litter-on-earth-day/

FACT: The initiative was organized by WSDOT Lab Testing Technician Denise Perez, who "partner[ed] with maintenance crews to organize the weeklong cleanup." *Source: WSDOT News Release, WSDOT, May 7, 2026. https://wsdot.wa.gov/about/news/2026/cleanup-aisle-i-5-crews-haul-away-litter-along-clark-county-highways-during-earth-week.

**What triggered it.** FACT: The cleanup was described as targeting "areas with increasing roadside debris." *Source: WSDOT News Release, WSDOT, May 7, 2026. https://wsdot.wa.gov/about/news/2026/cleanup-aisle-i-5-crews-haul-away-litter-along-clark-county-highways-during-earth-week. FACT: The March 2025 Clark County Today reporting documented community frustration, with Vancouver's mayor stating publicly: "If it's not between the white lines, it's low priority. Unless it's a safety hazard in the lane, low priority." *Source: Clark County Today, March 28, 2025. https://www.clarkcountytoday.com/news/wsdot-responds-to-complaints-about-litter-along-area-freeways-highways/

Analytical inference: The Earth Week cleanup is a responsive measure to documented community and political pressure about litter accumulation on Clark County highways — pressure that had been publicly voiced at a community forum as recently as March 2025 — rather than a routine maintenance event, suggesting the initiative carries a reputational and political dimension beyond standard operations.

**The $37K staff time figure.** *Specific dollar figure not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-07.* The $37K staff time investment referenced in the item title does not appear verbatim in any of the provided source blocks. It cannot be span-grounded and is therefore not confirmed as a FACT in this brief.

---

# Who's Driving It and What They Want

FACT: WSDOT "does not have dedicated litter crews." *Source: WSDOT News Release, WSDOT, May 7, 2026. https://wsdot.wa.gov/about/news/2026/cleanup-aisle-i-5-crews-haul-away-litter-along-clark-county-highways-during-earth-week. FACT: "During routine maintenance work such as guardrail repairs, drainage clearing and pothole patching, crews also remove debris from the roadside." *Source: WSDOT News Release, WSDOT, May 7, 2026. https://wsdot.wa.gov/about/news/2026/cleanup-aisle-i-5-crews-haul-away-litter-along-clark-county-highways-during-earth-week.

FACT: "Funding for litter cleanup competes in the same bucket of money as other critical maintenance work like repairing guardrails and potholes, striping roadways, maintaining safe rest areas, and responding to weather events including snow and ice conditions. This available budget is already very tight." *Source: Clark County Today, March 28, 2025. https://www.clarkcountytoday.com/news/wsdot-responds-to-complaints-about-litter-along-area-freeways-highways/

FACT: "WSDOT and the Department of Ecology spend more $17 million annually on litter pickup and removal activities statewide." *Source: WSDOT Litter on State Highways page, WSDOT, 2025. https://wsdot.wa.gov/construction-planning/protecting-environment/litter-state-highways.

FACT: "In 2025, WSDOT collected and disposed of 726 tons of trash from state highways across Washington." *Source: WSDOT Litter on State Highways page, WSDOT, 2025. https://wsdot.wa.gov/construction-planning/protecting-environment/litter-state-highways.

FACT: The WSDOT Southwest Region "stopped using crews from Corrections when the Larch Mountain Corrections Center closed in 2023." *Source: Clark County Today, March 28, 2025. https://www.clarkcountytoday.com/news/wsdot-responds-to-complaints-about-litter-along-area-freeways-highways/

Analytical inference: The combination of a corrections-crew gap since 2023 and a constrained budget has left Clark County corridors disproportionately dependent on volunteer programs and ad hoc staff mobilization, making them more susceptible to visible debris accumulation than regions where corrections crews still operate — a structural resourcing gap, not simply a seasonal backlog.

Analytical inference: The Adopt-A-Highway program and the Washington Department of Ecology litter corps (the latter covering the Southwest Region under coordinator Maggie Gray per the Ecology litter pickup page) are the primary supplemental mechanisms, but their combined output falls structurally short of the litter accumulation rate noted in ecology data.

---

# Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

FACT: Washington Department of Ecology's 2022 litter study found "more than 26 million pounds of litter accumulates along Washington's roads and interchanges every year." *Source: Washington State Department of Ecology, Litter Pickup page, 2022 litter study citation. https://ecology.wa.gov/waste-toxics/solid-waste-litter/litter/litter-pickup.

FACT: "In 2025, litter-pickup programs we funded collected more than 4.6 million pounds (2,337 tons) of litter and cleaned 22,848 miles of road, statewide." *Source: Washington State Department of Ecology, Litter Pickup page. https://ecology.wa.gov/waste-toxics/solid-waste-litter/litter/litter-pickup.

Analytical inference: The gap between 26 million pounds accumulating annually and 4.6 million pounds collected (approximately 18%) indicates that Washington's highway litter problem is structurally under-resourced at the state level — a gap that is not closed by episodic Earth Week cleanups regardless of scale.

Analytical inference: Conversion from episodic event to sustained funded program would likely require either a legislative budget allocation specifically for dedicated litter crews, or an expansion of the Ecology Litter Corps and corrections-program equivalents in the Southwest Region. Neither is announced in the sourced materials as of the date of this brief.

Operational implication: For freight operations using I-5, I-205, SR 14, SR 500, and SR 503 in Clark County, the structural maintenance gap means road condition deterioration from unsecured load debris and litter accumulation is a recurring, not exceptional, operating environment. Load-securing compliance — which directly affects debris generation on these corridors — is the freight sector's most direct touchpoint with this issue.

---

# Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

Operational implication: For workspaces operating road freight on the Clark County corridor (I-5/I-205 between Portland and southwest Washington), debris-related incidents (tire punctures, cargo damage, vehicle swerving hazards) represent an ongoing operational risk that is not diminishing under current WSDOT resourcing levels.

Operational implication: The WSDOT statement that crews "prioritize work that keeps roads open and safe, focusing first on larger hazards like furniture or bags of garbage from unsecured loads or illegal dumping" indicates that freight-generated roadside hazards (unsecured loads) receive higher response priority than general litter, which is operationally relevant for workspaces moving overland cargo through these corridors via contracted carriers.

FACT: WSDOT and Ecology "spend more $17 million annually on litter pickup and removal activities statewide." *Source: WSDOT Litter on State Highways page, WSDOT, 2025. https://wsdot.wa.gov/construction-planning/protecting-environment/litter-state-highways. Analytical inference: Budget competition between litter cleanup and safety-critical maintenance (guardrails, potholes, striping) means that when state transportation budgets face pressure, litter-specific activities are the first to be deferred, not safety repairs — a prioritization structure that is unlikely to change without dedicated litter-crew funding lines.

*Specific cost impact to road freight operations from debris-related incidents on Clark County corridors is not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-07. Effect on road freight verticals requires carrier-specific incident data.*

---

# Competitive Implications

No content for this section as of 2026-06-07: The sourced material does not contain information about competitor freight operators, freight-specific commercial positioning, or differential competitive access related to this highway maintenance event. Populating this section would require invention.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

For workspaces whose clients ship or receive freight on the I-5/I-205/SR 14/SR 500/SR 503 corridors in Clark County:

**What the workspace can credibly say:**
- WSDOT conducted a targeted Earth Week cleanup of Clark County freight corridors in April 2026, removing 3,410 pounds of debris — a concrete output from a documented initiative, not an aspirational claim.
- The state's own data shows litter pickup addresses roughly 18% of annual accumulation statewide, meaning road condition on these corridors will reflect ongoing accumulation between dedicated cleanup events.
- WSDOT does not maintain dedicated litter crews; cleanup is embedded in routine maintenance scheduling, so response intervals are variable.

**Claims to avoid:**
- Do not represent the Earth Week cleanup as evidence that the Clark County corridor litter problem has been resolved or that conditions are materially improved for the season — the sourced data does not support that conclusion.
- Do not cite the $37K staff time figure without confirming it against a primary WSDOT source; it does not appear in the provided source blocks.
- Do not represent WSDOT's Adopt-A-Highway or Ecology programs as equivalent to dedicated litter crews — WSDOT itself states it does not have dedicated litter crews.

**Question to pose to carrier partners operating these corridors:**
- What is your current load-securing protocol for outbound freight on I-5/I-205 in Clark County, and how are unsecured-load incidents reported and tracked? (This is the freight sector's direct compliance interface with the debris-generation cycle documented here.)

---

# What the Workspace Should Do Now

**MONITORING — re-check window: Q4 2026**

1. **Track WSDOT Southwest Region maintenance budget decisions in Washington's 2025–2027 biennium transportation budget.** If dedicated litter crew funding is added or Corrections-program equivalents are restored in Clark County, corridor conditions may improve materially. If not, the structural gap documented in March 2025 persists.

2. **Brief contracted road carriers operating Clark County lanes** on WSDOT's stated prioritization: unsecured load hazards receive higher response priority than ambient litter. Carriers should confirm load-securing protocols are current for I-5/I-205 operations — both as a compliance matter and because freight-generated roadside debris is the highest-priority category WSDOT responds to, meaning the workspace's supply chain is directly connected to the debris-generation dynamic these initiatives target.

3. **Note for ESG/sustainability reporting:** If the workspace reports on road freight environmental performance or community impact, WSDOT's documented investment ($17M annually statewide, Earth Week initiative in Clark County) provides verifiable sourced context for the regulatory and operational environment on US West Coast road lanes. This is not an operational action but may be relevant for client-facing sustainability documentation.

4. **Monitor WSDOT's 2026 Freight System Plan publication (expected fall 2026).** FACT: "The 2026 Freight System Plan is expected to be published in fall 2026." *Source: WSDOT Freight System Plan page, WSDOT, 2025–2026. https://wsdot.wa.gov/construction-planning/statewide-plans/freight-rail-plans/freight-system-plan. The plan "will incorporate a supply chain–focused lens" and addresses "mitigating freight system negative impacts on communities" — language that could encompass debris and litter impacts generated by freight operations. Analytical inference: If the 2026 plan includes freight-specific litter or load-securing recommendations, it may generate new operational guidance or compliance expectations for carriers on Washington state highways.

prior.title: Metro Nashville Energy Sustainability Program and Energy Savings Revolving Fund

prior.full_brief:
# Metro Nashville Energy Sustainability Program and Energy Savings Revolving Fund

**Format:** Market Signal Brief | **Severity:** MONITORING | **Signal Band:** Corporate

---

## Context Note

This brief covers a municipal government initiative — Metro Nashville's Energy Savings Program and Energy Savings Revolving Fund — operated within Metro Nashville's Department of General Services. The workspace is a global freight forwarding operation. The initiative is not a direct regulatory obligation on private freight operators. Its relevance is indirect: as a signal of municipal sustainability infrastructure maturity, as a model for public-sector client engagement, and as context for understanding the operating environment for facilities-intensive logistics operations serving Nashville-area clients and government accounts. All direct claims are grounded in the five source blocks provided. Sections where no sourced facts exist are omitted per the Integrity Rule.

---

# What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING

Metro Nashville's Department of General Services established an "Energy Savings Program" and an "Energy Savings Revolving Fund" as part of a package of sustainability initiatives announced in December 2019. The trigger was the signing of the Global Covenant of Mayors and a suite of three pieces of "Green New Deal" legislation passed by Metro Council in 2019.

- **FACT:** "In 2020, Metro established an 'Energy Savings Program' housed within the Department of General Services to support energy efficiency efforts in Metro's general government facilities with the goal of achieving at least 20% reductions in energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions as well as substantial costs." *Source: Sustainability (Mayor's Office), Metropolitan Government of Nashville, undated (current as of 2025). https://www.nashville.gov/departments/mayor/sustainability-resilience-environment/sustainability

- **FACT:** "General Services also manages an 'Energy Savings Revolving Fund' deploying ongoing measurement and tracking of energy savings projects for most Metro department facilities and began working across multiple Metro real estate portfolios to engage in energy benchmarking." *Source: Sustainability (Mayor's Office), Metropolitan Government of Nashville, undated. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/mayor/sustainability-resilience-environment/sustainability

- **FACT:** The Energy Savings Program was among "Mayor Cooper's initial climate change and sustainability initiatives" announced December 4, 2019, described as "the creation of an 'Energy Savings Program' to support energy efficiency efforts in Metro's general government facilities." *Source: Mayor Cooper Announces Multiple Initiatives to Combat Climate Change and Promote Sustainability, Metropolitan Government of Nashville, December 4, 2019. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/mayor/news/mayor-cooper-announces-multiple-initiatives-combat-climate-change-and-promote

- **FACT:** The three 2019 legislative triggers were Metro Code 2.32.065 Fleet Electrification, Metro Code 2.32.070 Building Retrofits and LEED requirements, and Metro Code 2.32.080 Renewable Portfolio Standard. *Source: About the Division of Sustainability, Department of General Services, Metropolitan Government of Nashville, updated December 30, 2025. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/general-services/sustainability/socket/about-socket

The program's scope is Metro government-owned facilities — not private sector facilities. As of the sources reviewed, the revolving fund mechanism has not been extended to private operators or freight facility tenants.

*Specific revolving fund capitalization amount, individual project loan sizes, and fund balance not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.*

---

# Who's Driving It and What They Want

- **FACT:** The initiative is housed within the Department of General Services Sustainability Division. "The Department of General Services Sustainability Division integrates sustainable practices throughout the department's programs and operations with the goal to reduce energy use, waste, and greenhouse gas emissions." *Source: About the Division of Sustainability, Department of General Services, Metropolitan Government of Nashville, updated December 30, 2025. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/general-services/sustainability/socket/about-socket

- **FACT:** The overarching policy target is "CO2-reduction targets for Metro Government will be 40 percent by 2030 and 80 percent by 2050." *Source: Mayor Cooper Announces Multiple Initiatives to Combat Climate Change and Promote Sustainability, Metropolitan Government of Nashville, December 4, 2019. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/mayor/news/mayor-cooper-announces-multiple-initiatives-combat-climate-change-and-promote

- **FACT:** "In February 2022, Metro Nashville Council passed a resolution committing to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by 80% from 2014 levels by 2050." *Source: About the Division of Sustainability, Department of General Services, Metropolitan Government of Nashville, updated December 30, 2025. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/general-services/sustainability/socket/about-socket

- **FACT:** The 2021 Climate Action Plan, produced by a 50-member Sustainability Advisory Committee, identifies decarbonization of the electrical grid, electrification of motor vehicles, and electrification (greening) of buildings as three key areas "that will result in the greatest carbon reductions and enable the City to reach its sustainability targets." *Source: Sustainability (Mayor's Office), Metropolitan Government of Nashville, undated. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/mayor/sustainability-resilience-environment/sustainability

- **FACT:** "Driving change in these three areas typically results in 60% to 70% greenhouse gas reductions and makes most cities' mitigation goals attainable." *Source: Sustainability (Mayor's Office), Metropolitan Government of Nashville, undated. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/mayor/sustainability-resilience-environment/sustainability

Analytical inference: The Department of General Services is seeking to demonstrate that public-sector energy efficiency investment is self-financing via the revolving fund structure — savings from completed projects re-capitalise the fund for future projects — a model the DOE Better Buildings toolkit identifies as a green revolving fund design pattern. This gives the program internal political sustainability independent of annual budget cycles.

---

# Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

MONITORING

The program is active and expanding. Key forward indicators from the sourced record:

- **FACT:** "Metro is also pursuing solar installations on its properties. Metro's total solar footprint across 30 installations currently totals 5.52 megawatts." *Source: Sustainability (Mayor's Office), Metropolitan Government of Nashville, undated. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/major/sustainability-resilience-environment/sustainability

- **FACT:** A two-year solar cost-benefit analysis "identified opportunities for deploying solar across its existing real estate portfolio... up to 235 properties that are suited for potential installations amounting to up to 100 megawatts of solar capacity. Energy savings from these installations could amount to $79 million dollars." *Source: Sustainability (Mayor's Office), Metropolitan Government of Nashville, undated. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/mayor/sustainability-resilience-environment/sustainability

- **FACT:** "Once operational in 2026, this [40 MW Green Invest] installation will account for about 13% of Metro's annual power supply." *Source: Sustainability (Mayor's Office), Metropolitan Government of Nashville, undated. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/mayor/sustainability-resilience-environment/sustainability

- **FACT:** In 2022, Metro Nashville conducted energy benchmarking across 335 facilities "representing 20.3 million square feet" with total energy consumption of "nearly 1.3 billion kBTUs." *Source: Metro Government Energy Benchmarking, Department of General Services, Metropolitan Government of Nashville, April 13, 2023. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/general-services/news/metro-government-energy-benchmarking

- **FACT:** Participating departments in the 2022 benchmarking program included Metro Nashville Public Schools, General Services, Farmers Market, Fairgrounds, Parks and Recreation, Metro Action Commission, Nashville Fire Department, Davidson County Sheriff's Office, WeGo, Nashville Department of Transportation, and Nashville Public Library. *Source: Metro Government Energy Benchmarking, Department of General Services, Metropolitan Government of Nashville, April 13, 2023. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/general-services/news/metro-government-energy-benchmarking

- **FACT:** "Most of these city entities had never systematically tracked their energy consumption before." *Source: Metro Government Energy Benchmarking, Department of General Services, Metropolitan Government of Nashville, April 13, 2023. https://www.nashville.gov/departments/general-services/news/metro-government-energy-benchmarking

- **FACT:** In 2023, Nashville adopted a local C-PACER program "which enables access to low-interest and long-term financing for commercial energy efficiency, renewable energy, and resiliency improvements." *Source: Nashville | ACEEE, American Council for an Energy-Efficient Economy, updated May 2024. https://database.aceee.org/city/nashville-tn

Analytical inference: The benchmarking exercise across 335 facilities is the data-collection phase that feeds the revolving fund's project prioritisation. Buildings with the highest EUI scores relative to national CBECS benchmarks are the most likely next candidates for retrofit financing through the fund. As the fund matures and more projects complete, the volume of energy savings data available to external clients and logistics tenants using Metro facilities will increase.

Conversion trigger: The program converts from a Metro-internal initiative to a broader commercial-environment signal if (a) the city adopts a mandatory commercial benchmarking program (ACEEE notes Nashville "is considering a commercial benchmarking program and is gathering stakeholder input" as of September 2023) or (b) the C-PACER financing mechanism attracts private commercial real estate projects adjacent to logistics corridors.

*Specific re-check window: Review at Q4 2025 for commercial benchmarking ordinance status, and at Q2 2026 for Green Invest 40 MW array operational confirmation.*

---

# Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

**Filtered by transport mode (air primary, road secondary, ocean tertiary) and relevant cargo verticals.**

*No direct cost obligation on private freight operators arises from this initiative as sourced. The following are labeled inferences.*

Analytical inference: For workspaces operating road-based drayage and last-mile delivery into Nashville Metro facilities — including public venues, government campuses, and LEED-certified buildings — the Energy Savings Program signals that facility managers at those sites are under active pressure to reduce energy use intensity. This translates operationally into: loading dock scheduling (off-peak delivery windows to support building energy management systems), potential requirements for low-emission vehicles on government-facility approaches as the fleet electrification mandate (Metro Code 2.32.065) extends to facility access rules, and tighter documentation of emissions associated with deliveries to government accounts.

Analytical inference: For workspaces handling cargo destined for Nashville-area event venues or government-contracted facilities, awareness of the C-PACER program is relevant to client conversations: commercial building owners accessing C-PACER financing for energy upgrades may reconfigure loading, storage, and cold-chain infrastructure as part of retrofit scopes, affecting logistics access and dwell-time planning.

Analytical inference: The benchmarking program's finding that energy consumption across 335 Metro facilities totalled nearly 1.3 billion kBTUs — equivalent to 17,000 households — contextualises the scale of the city's operational footprint. For workspaces with government freight accounts in Nashville, this scale of municipal facility management indicates a procurement environment increasingly oriented toward sustainability criteria in vendor selection.

*Specific energy cost figures for freight operators at Nashville facilities not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-14.*

---

# Competitive Implications

*No sourced competitor positioning specific to freight operators in Nashville is available from the source blocks provided.*

Analytical inference: Workspaces that can demonstrate Scope 1 and Scope 3 emissions tracking aligned with the energy benchmarking methodology Metro Nashville uses (ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager, EUI metrics, kBTU-based consumption accounting) are positioned to respond credibly when Nashville government procurement processes incorporate emissions disclosure criteria. The benchmarking program's use of ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager as the tracking platform is a specific alignment point.

Analytical inference: The ACEEE City Scorecard ranks Nashville 33rd nationally with a score of 70.50 out of 250. Analytical inference: This mid-tier ranking suggests Nashville's commercial sustainability requirements for private operators remain less mature than leading cities (e.g., those with mandatory commercial benchmarking), providing a window in which proactive sustainability positioning by the workspace creates differentiation before requirements harden.

Industry interpretation: The DOE Better Buildings Revolving Loan Funds Toolkit identifies municipal green revolving funds as replicable models that often expand from government buildings to include utilities and commercial partners over time. Nashville's program is currently government-internal; whether it expands is not determinable from available sources.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- Metro Nashville has an active, legislatively grounded energy efficiency program covering over 335 government facilities and 20.3 million square feet — one of the more systematically documented municipal programs in the Southeast. *Sourced.*
- The program targets at least 20% reductions in energy consumption and GHG emissions across Metro general government facilities, with a government-wide GHG target of 80% reduction from 2014 levels by 2050. *Sourced.*
- Nashville adopted a C-PACER commercial financing program in 2023, meaning commercial property owners — including warehouse and logistics facility operators — have access to long-term, low-interest financing for energy efficiency and renewable energy upgrades. *Sourced.*
- A 40 MW solar array is expected to be operational in 2026, accounting for approximately 13% of Metro's annual power supply. *Sourced.*

**What to avoid claiming:**

- Do not claim the revolving fund is available to private operators — no sourced evidence supports this.
- Do not claim Nashville has mandatory commercial benchmarking — the city is in stakeholder consultation as of September 2023 but has not adopted an ordinance.
- Do not claim specific emissions reductions have been achieved under the program — no performance data for the revolving fund's completed projects appears in the sourced record.
- Do not cite the ACEEE 70.50 score as a "leading" position — it reflects 33rd place out of scored cities at 250 points maximum.

**Questions to pose to Nashville-area government clients:**

- Is your facility covered by the Metro benchmarking program, and do you have an EUI baseline we should be aware of for logistics planning?
- Has your building applied for C-PACER financing, and are any energy retrofits planned that would affect loading dock or cold-storage access?
- Does your procurement policy currently include emissions criteria for logistics vendors, and what is the timeline for formalising those criteria?

---

# What the Workspace Should Do Now

MONITORING

1. **Track the Nashville commercial benchmarking ordinance.** ACEEE records that Nashville is considering mandatory commercial benchmarking and gathering stakeholder input as of September 2023. *Re-check at Q4 2025.* If adopted, logistics facilities operating in Davidson County could face disclosure obligations that intersect with the workspace's Scope 3 reporting posture. No action required yet; establish a monitoring trigger.

2. **Evaluate C-PACER as a client conversation tool for Nashville warehouse or event-venue accounts.** The 2023 C-PACER program enables commercial property owners to finance energy upgrades at low cost. For clients operating Nashville facilities where the workspace stores or handles cargo, the workspace can raise awareness of available financing and position itself as a logistics partner that understands the sustainability upgrade cycle — without overclaiming involvement in the program itself.

3. **Align internal emissions data formats with ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager metrics.** Metro Nashville's benchmarking program uses ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager and EUI (kBTU/sq ft) as the standard. Analytical inference: workspaces that can report their Nashville facility energy consumption in these units will be better positioned to respond to any future government-account vendor sustainability disclosure request without re-working data formats under deadline pressure.

4. **Monitor Green Invest 40 MW array operational date (expected 2026).** Once operational, this installation will shift Metro Nashville's power supply mix — approximately 13% toward carbon-free generation. Analytical inference: this changes the Scope 2 emissions factor for any Nashville Metro facility served by Nashville Electric Service (NES), which is relevant to workspaces calculating market-based Scope 2 emissions for Nashville operations.

5. **Do not invest in Nashville sustainability positioning claims beyond what is sourced.** The revolving fund's financial performance, completed project list, and cumulative savings have not been publicly reported in the sourced record. Claims beyond the program's stated design and targets risk being uncorroborated if challenged by a client or counterparty.

prior.title: Interstate 10 Kino to Country Club Project: Active Design-Build Construction (2024-2028)
prior.summary: Arizona Department of Transportation is executing a major design-build highway reconstruction project on Interstate 10 in Tucson between mileposts 262 (east of Park Avenue) and Alvernon Way. Awarded to Sundt-Jacobs, the project includes three general purpose lanes per direction, interchange reconstructions at Kino Parkway and Country Club Road, and an I-10 undercrossing for the Kino Sports Complex. Construction is underway with completion targeted for Summer 2028.
prior.full_brief:
# Interstate 10 Kino to Country Club Project: Active Design-Build Construction (2024–2028)

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING

The Arizona Department of Transportation (ADOT) is executing the largest highway construction project in southern Arizona history on the I-10 corridor between Kino Parkway and Country Club Road in Tucson. The project is currently in active construction.

**Project scale and contract award:** FACT — "On Friday, Aug. 16, the State Transportation Board awarded the design-build project to Sundt-Jacobs. At $600 million, it will be the largest highway construction project in southern Arizona history." *Source: ADOT News Release, Arizona Department of Transportation, August 16, 2024. https://azdot.gov/news/contractor-approved-upcoming-i-10-reconstruction-tucson

**Construction start confirmed:** FACT — "The Sundt-Jacobs team began construction on the I-10: Kino to Country Club project in Tucson for the Arizona Department of Transportation." *Source: Sundt Construction Press Release, Sundt Construction, Inc., August 28, 2025. https://www.sundt.com/2025/construction-starts-on-southern-arizonas-largest-highway-project/

**Scheduled completion:** FACT — "The project is currently in construction and scheduled to be completed in summer 2028." *Source: ADOT Project Page, Arizona Department of Transportation, accessed 2025. https://azdot.gov/projects/southcentral-district-projects/Southcentral/interstate-10-kino-country-club

**Programme origin:** FACT — "The Interstate 10: Kino to Country Club project is the first project to come out of the Interstate 10 and State Route 210 Transportation Design Concept Report (DCR) that was completed in 2020." *Source: ADOT Project Page, Arizona Department of Transportation, accessed 2025. https://azdot.gov/projects/southcentral-district-projects/Southcentral/interstate-10-kino-country-club

**Capital programme placement:** FACT — "The I-10: Kino to Country Club project was programmed into the 2024-2028 Five-Year Transportation Facilities Construction Program as a Design/Build in FY 25." *Source: ADOT Project Page, Arizona Department of Transportation, accessed 2025. https://azdot.gov/projects/southcentral-district-projects/Southcentral/interstate-10-kino-country-club

**Construction phase as of August 2025:** FACT — "For the first phase of the project this summer, the team has focused on a 'make-ready' phase, including construction of a new westbound on-ramp at the Alvernon interchange and re-configuring the Kino Parkway ramps. Next month, the project team will begin construction in the median, including a 60-foot-wide pavement section with median barrier for eastbound and westbound traffic." *Source: Sundt Construction Press Release, Sundt Construction, Inc., August 28, 2025. https://www.sundt.com/2025/construction-starts-on-southern-arizonas-largest-highway-project/

**What triggered it:** FACT — The project addresses stated deficiencies in the corridor: "Address traffic congestion / Improve safety / Increase traffic flow / Improve traffic interchanges." *Source: ADOT Project Page, Arizona Department of Transportation, accessed 2025. https://azdot.gov/projects/southcentral-district-projects/Southcentral/interstate-10-kino-country-club

Operational implication: The I-10 Kino to Country Club segment is a principal surface artery for road freight moving through Tucson's urban core, connecting to the broader I-10 corridor that runs from the California ports to Texas. A $600 million reconstruction and widening programme spanning approximately three years introduces structured, extended disruption to this segment that freight-dependent workspaces with road operations in the southern Arizona corridor cannot treat as a short-term event.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**State agency ownership:** FACT — The project is a State Transportation Board contract, with ADOT as the project owner. *Source: ADOT News Release, Arizona Department of Transportation, August 16, 2024. https://azdot.gov/news/contractor-approved-upcoming-i-10-reconstruction-tucson

**Design-build team:** FACT — "Serving as lead designer on the Kino to Country Club project in Tucson, representing the largest interstate highway design-build and freeway construction project in the region." Jacobs serves as lead designer; Sundt Construction as general contractor, forming the Sundt-Jacobs joint venture. *Source: Jacobs Newsroom, Jacobs Solutions Inc., accessed 2025. https://www.jacobs.com/newsroom/news/jacobs-designing-southern-arizonas-largest-highway-construction-project

**Stated contractor profile:** FACT — Sundt Construction is "one of the country's largest and most respected general contractors" and "is owned entirely by its 4,000 employees," currently "ranked the country's 46th largest construction company by ENR." *Source: Sundt Construction Press Release, Sundt Construction, Inc., August 28, 2025. https://www.sundt.com/2025/construction-starts-on-southern-arizonas-largest-highway-project/

**Broader corridor programme intent:** FACT — "The project will be the first to result from a study completed in 2020 that recommended how to best improve mobility along a roughly 10-mile stretch of I-10 from I-19 to Kolb Road. The study also identified an alignment for an extension of State Route 210 (Barraza Aviation Parkway) along Alvernon Way that will connect I-10 to downtown Tucson." *Source: ADOT News Release, Arizona Department of Transportation, August 16, 2024. https://azdot.gov/news/contractor-approved-upcoming-i-10-reconstruction-tucson

Analytical inference: ADOT's objective is durable capacity and interchange safety improvement, not a temporary maintenance event. The 2020 DCR scope — covering a ten-mile stretch and identifying a future SR 210 extension — signals that this project is Phase 1 of a multi-phase corridor programme, not a standalone intervention. Workspaces relying on this corridor should plan for sequential phases beyond 2028.

**Arizona State Freight Plan context:** ADOT is concurrently updating its Arizona State Freight Plan (2022 plan being revised; stakeholder meetings held October–November 2025). FACT — "The Arizona State Freight Plan is currently updating the previous plan (approved December 2022). This plan establishes immediate and long-range plans for freight related transportation investments." *Source: Arizona State Freight Plan 2026 Update, Arizona Department of Transportation, accessed 2025. https://azdot.gov/planning/transportation-programs/arizona-state-freight-plan

Analytical inference: The concurrent freight plan update and the I-10 Kino to Country Club construction represent overlapping policy and infrastructure signals for the same corridor. The freight plan update's outcome (expected to be approved as the 2026 plan) may identify additional I-10 investments downstream of this project, reinforcing the case for treating the current construction as one episode in a longer programme.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Scheduled completion:** FACT — "The project is anticipated to continue through 2028." *Source: Sundt Construction Press Release, Sundt Construction, Inc., August 28, 2025. https://www.sundt.com/2025/construction-starts-on-southern-arizonas-largest-highway-project/

**Phased construction sequence already underway:** FACT — The make-ready phase (Alvernon on-ramp, Kino Parkway ramp reconfiguration) began summer 2025; median construction including a "60-foot-wide pavement section with median barrier" was scheduled to begin the following month (September 2025). *Source: Sundt Construction Press Release, Sundt Construction, Inc., August 28, 2025. https://www.sundt.com/2025/construction-starts-on-southern-arizonas-largest-highway-project/

Analytical inference: The project follows a standard design-build highway phasing model: make-ready and ramp reconfiguration first, then mainline median work, then bridge structures, then interchange reconstruction. Each phase typically shifts lane configurations and access points, meaning disruption to the corridor is not static — it evolves as the project sequence advances through 2028.

**Subsequent corridor phases:** Analytical inference — The 2020 DCR covers approximately ten miles from I-19 to Kolb Road. The Kino to Country Club segment addresses one portion. Additional corridor segments identified in the DCR (including the SR 210 / Barraza Aviation Parkway extension alignment) represent potential future programme phases, the timing of which has not been publicly announced from the sources available. Re-check window: monitor ADOT project announcements and the 2026 Arizona State Freight Plan final publication for next-phase programming.

**Freight plan conversion trigger:** FACT — Arizona State Freight Plan 2026 update stakeholder meetings were held in October and November 2025; the update is in progress. *Source: Arizona State Freight Plan 2026 Update, Arizona Department of Transportation, accessed 2025. https://azdot.gov/planning/transportation-programs/arizona-state-freight-plan Analytical inference: Publication of the 2026 plan is the proximate trigger for understanding how ADOT prioritises further I-10 corridor investments in the Five-Year Construction Program beyond FY 2028.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

**Applicable workspace modes:** This signal is most material for workspaces with road freight operations transiting the Tucson urban I-10 corridor. Air operations at Tucson International Airport have indirect exposure via surface access roads. Ocean and rail operations are not directly affected by this corridor segment.

**Interchange changes with direct access implications:**

FACT — The project removes the Palo Verde Road interchange: "Remove the existing interchange Palo Verde Road." *Source: ADOT Project Page, Arizona Department of Transportation, accessed 2025. https://azdot.gov/projects/southcentral-district-projects/Southcentral/interstate-10-kino-country-club

FACT — A replacement Country Club Road interchange is under construction: "Construct a new traffic interchange at I-10 and Country Club Road that will provide access for traffic that currently uses the interchange at Palo Verde Road to allow for safer traffic movements to and from I-10." *Source: ADOT News Release, Arizona Department of Transportation, August 16, 2024. https://azdot.gov/news/contractor-approved-upcoming-i-10-reconstruction-tucson

Operational implication: Freight vehicles currently using the Palo Verde Road on/off ramps for last-mile access to Tucson distribution points, customer sites, or staging areas will need to reroute during construction and permanently adapt to the Country Club Road interchange once the project completes. Drivers and route-planning systems should be updated to reflect this structural change, not just a temporary closure.

**Capacity expansion post-completion:**

FACT — The completed project will include "three general purpose lanes in each direction between milepost 262 (east of Park Avenue) and Alvernon Way" and widen "to four lanes in each direction west of Kino Parkway." *Source: ADOT Project Page, Arizona Department of Transportation, accessed 2025. https://azdot.gov/projects/southcentral-district-projects/Southcentral/interstate-10-kino-country-club

Operational implication: The post-2028 corridor will carry significantly greater vehicle throughput. For workspaces with time-critical road moves (live events, high-value automotive, film and TV equipment) through Tucson, this represents a long-term reduction in congestion-related delay risk on this segment — but the three-year construction window is the near-term exposure.

**Construction-period disruption costs:** *Specific construction-period surcharges, lane closure schedules, or freight delay cost estimates not available from primary sources as of 2025-06-10.* Workspaces should request current lane closure schedules from ADOT (project line: 855.712.8530 or i10kinotocc@azdot.gov) to assess timing-sensitive move risk.

**Urban corridor freight context:** Analytical inference — I-10 through Tucson is part of the principal freight corridor linking the California ports, the Phoenix metropolitan area, and Texas. Disruption on the Kino to Country Club segment does not close the interstate, but interchange reconfiguration and lane shifts during active construction phases increase transit time variability and raise the probability of delay for time-critical cargo moves. Workspaces with guaranteed-delivery obligations on Tucson-routed lanes should build contingency time into schedules for moves transiting this segment before summer 2028.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

Analytical inference: For freight forwarders and carriers with established southern Arizona road operations, the ability to proactively communicate construction-period routing alternatives and revised ETA models to clients is a differentiator during the 2025–2028 window. Operators who absorb the disruption passively and pass delays to clients without explanation will be exposed commercially; operators who brief clients ahead of each construction phase change can position this as value-added service intelligence.

Analytical inference: The permanent removal of the Palo Verde Road interchange and the introduction of the Country Club Road interchange will require updates to routing profiles, driver briefings, and GPS/TMS configurations. Workspaces that update these systems ahead of interchange activation — rather than after the fact — avoid the operational friction and client-facing delivery misses that lagging competitors will experience.

Analytical inference: The broader DCR programme (ten-mile corridor, potential SR 210 extension) positions ADOT as a continuing infrastructure investor in this corridor through at least the late 2020s. Workspaces with long-term southern Arizona client commitments may find it commercially useful to demonstrate awareness of the programme's multi-phase nature when presenting capability to clients in the Tucson market.

*No sourced competitor-specific positioning data is available from the source pool for this item. Named competitor operational responses have not been reported in the sources provided.*

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- The I-10 Kino to Country Club project is a confirmed, funded, active design-build construction programme awarded to Sundt-Jacobs at $600 million — the largest highway construction project in southern Arizona history. Construction started in August 2025 and is scheduled to complete in summer 2028.
- The project permanently removes the Palo Verde Road interchange and replaces it with a new Country Club Road interchange. Any client with facilities, distribution points, or delivery addresses that currently access I-10 via Palo Verde Road should be advised now to plan for permanent rerouting.
- The workspace monitors ADOT project updates and can provide advance notice of construction phase changes affecting route timing on this corridor.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- Do you have facilities, customer delivery addresses, or staging locations accessed via the Palo Verde Road or Kino Parkway interchanges? If so, we should review your routing profile now.
- Do any of your time-critical Tucson-routed shipments have guaranteed delivery windows that would be affected by construction-period delay variability on this segment?
- Are you aware that this project is Phase 1 of a larger ten-mile I-10 / SR 210 corridor improvement programme, and that further phases may follow after 2028?

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not assert specific lane closure dates, detour routes, or access window guarantees without first confirming with ADOT's project team — construction sequencing in a design-build contract can shift from published schedules.
- Do not represent the post-2028 capacity improvement as eliminating congestion on the broader I-10 Tucson corridor; the improvement covers a specific segment and does not address the full ten-mile corridor identified in the 2020 DCR.
- Do not cite construction cost as a freight surcharge basis without a sourced rate structure from the relevant carrier.

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**ACTION 1 — Audit Tucson-routed road freight for interchange exposure.** Identify all active shipment lanes, delivery addresses, and staging facilities that currently access I-10 via the Palo Verde Road interchange. This interchange is being permanently removed. Update routing profiles in TMS and driver instruction sets before the interchange is deactivated. Scope: road freight operations transiting the Kino–Country Club segment. Why now: construction is active and interchange reconfiguration is already underway as of August 2025.

**ACTION 2 — Subscribe to ADOT project alerts for construction phase changes.** ADOT provides email project updates and a dedicated information line (855.712.8530) and project email (i10kinotocc@azdot.gov). *Source: ADOT Project Page, Arizona Department of Transportation, accessed 2025. https://azdot.gov/projects/southcentral-district-projects/Southcentral/interstate-10-kino-country-club Register to receive phase change notices ahead of each major construction transition (median work, bridge construction, interchange demolition/build). This provides lead time to brief drivers and clients before disruption, not after.

**ACTION 3 — Build construction contingency into Tucson-corridor SLA commitments.** For time-critical cargo verticals (live events, high-value automotive, film and TV) with Tucson routing, insert explicit construction-period variability language into client agreements and booking confirmations for road legs transiting this segment. Scope: all guaranteed-delivery commitments on I-10 Tucson routing. Deadline: before the next major construction phase intensifies (median and bridge work, scheduled to begin September 2025 per the August 28, 2025 press release).

**ACTION 4 — Monitor the Arizona State Freight Plan 2026 Update for downstream corridor programming.** The freight plan update in progress (stakeholder meetings October–November 2025) will define ADOT's next investment priorities. If additional I-10 corridor phases are programmed into the next Five-Year Construction Program, the disruption timeline for southern Arizona road operations extends beyond 2028. Re-check window: monitor for the 2026 plan publication; expected within the 2026 calendar year based on the update cycle. *Source: Arizona State Freight Plan 2026 Update, Arizona Department of Transportation, accessed 2025. https://azdot.gov/planning/transportation-programs/arizona-state-freight-plan

**ACTION 5 — Brief commercial teams on the multi-phase corridor programme.** Clients with Tucson-area supply chains will benefit from being told that this is Phase 1 of a ten-mile corridor study, not a standalone project. This positions the workspace as a proactive partner with programme-level awareness, not a reactive logistics provider.

---
